An EF-1 tornado with peak winds estimated to be 105 mph and a path width of 90 yards tracked for 3.15 miles through Boone County. The tornado lifted at 10:56 PM. The tornado caused damage to industrial buildings with extensive wall and roof damage observed. Some of this damage occurred at the Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport. A number of trees were uprooted or snapped along the path.
Deck Awning lifted up and carried.

An EF-0 Tornado with estimated peak winds of about 80 mph and a path width of about 150 yards touched down just north of New Miami near Hamilton Eaton Road and traveled a total of 5.9 miles, lifting at about 8:50PM. The tornado caused damage to mobile homes, roof damage to single family homes, and damage to numerous trees and some power poles.
Roof shingles taken off portable shelters.
Poles ripped apart, fence down, yard furniture everywhere. Time estimated from RADAR.

An EF-0 tornado developed in West Chester and traveled east into Warren County, through Mason and Kings Mills before ending about 2 miles west of Morrow at 906 PM. Numerous trees were downed along its path, along with sporadic minor house damage.

An EF-0 tornado with estimated peak winds between 80 and 85 mph and a path width of about 150 yards touched down near Waynesville Road just west of the Little Miami River and continued east crossing Corwin Road and on to ONeall Road and the Caesar Creek Visitor Center. Numerous hardwood trees were snapped or uprooted, with significant damage to a barn. Path length was 3.05 miles.

An EF0 tornado was confirmed in Shelby County, OH from storms that occurred the evening of July 19th, 2025, based on video and drone footage evidence. Damage to crops and minor tree damage were observed northeast of Anna, OH.
Damage to crops near Lochard Road and Wells Road intersection. Possible tornado. Time estimated from RADAR.

An EF0 tornado was confirmed in Clark County, OH from the storms that occurred the evening of July 19, 2025 based on NWS survey results. The primary damage with this tornado was located southeast of North Hampton where large, broken tree limbs were observed, along with extensive corn field damage on both sides of Shrine Rd.

An EF1 tornado was confirmed in Clark County, OH from the storms that occurred the evening of July 19, 2025 based on NWS survey results. The most extensive damage was observed in the town of Northridge, where two homes had large portions of their roofs removed, with wind speeds estimated to 100 MPH.

An EFU tornado was confirmed using videos from social media, broadcast media, and local officials. The tornado was contained within heavily forested areas, limiting access to ground surveys. While the tornado is being confirmed with unknown strength, the location has been approximated based on radar and geolocation techniques.
